sources.name: Speedrun.com — Disney's Aladdin (Genesis) / Guides
sources.notes: Confirms: skid boost mechanic (restart run during skidding = brief speed boost). Sword swing bug after landing from certain sections.

sources.name: Speed Demos Archive — Disney's Aladdin Genesis

notes: NOT THE SAME AS SNES ALADDIN — the Genesis version (Virgin/Sega) and SNES version (Capcom) are completely different games.
Genesis version uses authentic Disney film frames rotoscoped into sprites — noted for animation quality.
SKID BOOST: Running, stopping, then immediately restarting during the skidding animation gives a brief speed boost.
SUPER JUMP: Unique to Genesis version — Aladdin can perform an extra-high jump (not present in Amiga/PC versions).
Sword swing has a long wind-up animation — sluggish for combat.
Apple throwing: the primary ranged attack, thrown horizontally to stun enemies.
Walk/run speed and jump values not precisely documented. Estimated from gameplay.

skid_boost.description: When running and stopping, Aladdin enters a skidding animation. If the run button is pressed again immediately during the skid, a brief speed boost is gained.
skid_boost.notes: Used in speedruns for faster horizontal traversal. The skid animation's early frames have a speed exploitation window.

super_jump.description: Extra-high jump — Aladdin can jump significantly higher than the standard jump height.
super_jump.availability: Genesis version only (not in Amiga/PC ports)
super_jump.source: Wikipedia: 'Both the Amiga and IBM PC versions lack a super jump that originally allowed Aladdin to leap extra high'
notes: The super jump allows reaching items and platforms out of reach of the standard jump.
It's unclear if this is a charged jump or a button-combo jump. VERIFY activation.
Sword swing has a 'strangely long wind-up animation that's extremely sluggish' — community consensus.
In guard encounters, often better to take damage than wait for the sword animation.
Bug: after landing from certain height sections, the sword temporarily stops working. Fix: jump or throw apple.

apple.description: Throwable projectile that stuns enemies on contact.
apple.notes: Faster to stun with apple than to wait for sword animation. Preferred for most enemy encounters.

magic_carpet.description: Aladdin rides the magic carpet in specific sections for aerial traversal.
magic_carpet.notes: Carpet sections have different physics — flying at controlled speed above the standard terrain.

genesis_vs_snes.art_style: Genesis: rotoscoped Disney film animation. SNES: hand-drawn sprites (different art style)
genesis_vs_snes.mechanics: Completely different game designs despite same source material
genesis_vs_snes.notes: These are two completely different games that happen to share the same IP and basic premise.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The Genesis Aladdin is the canonical example of 'rotoscoped animation in games' — actual Disney film frames converted to sprites. The animation quality is exceptional for 1993 but the gameplay consequence is that the animations are long (the sword wind-up) and can't be cancelled.

The skid boost is an unintentional mechanic discovered by speedrunners — the game's animation system creates a speed window in the transition between running and stopping. This is a great example of emergent physics from animation state machines.

The sword's sluggish wind-up creates a risk-reward decision in combat: wait for the animation to complete (time-costly) or use apples (resource-costly). This forces resource management even in a simple platformer context.

The Genesis/SNES split is a famous example of licensed game bifurcation — same IP, two completely different teams, two completely different games. The Genesis version is generally considered more polished while the SNES version is more action-game-like.
